subject: Mathematics
q: Let $f(x)=\int_0^t t\left(t^2-9 t+20\right) d t, 1 \leq x \leq 5$. If the range of $f$ is $[\alpha, \beta]$, then $4(\alpha+\beta)$ equals :
question: Let $f(x)=\int_0^t t\left(t^2-9 t+20\right) d t, 1 \leq x \leq 5$. If the range of $f$ is $[\alpha, \beta]$, then $4(\alpha+\beta)$ equals :
solution:
$f^{\prime}(x)=x\left(x^2-9 x+20\right), x \in(1,5)$ $=(x-4) x(x-5)$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)>0 \forall x \in(1,4) \\ & \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x) < 0 \forall x \in(4,5) \\ & \Rightarrow f(x) \text { increasing in }(1,4) \\ & \quad f(x) \text { decreasing in }(4,5) \\ & \Rightarrow \text { critical points to check: }\end{aligned}$ $\begin{aligned} & x=1,4,5 \\ & f(x)=\int_0^x\left(t^3-9 t^2+20 t\right) d t \\ & =\frac{t^4}{4}-3 t^3+\left.10 t^2\right|_0 ^x=\frac{x^4}{4}-3 x^3+10 x^2 \\ & f(1)=\frac{1}{4}-3+10=\frac{29}{4} \\ & f(4)=4^3-3.4^3+10.4^2=-2.4^3+10.4^2=32 \\ & f(5)=\frac{5^4}{4}-3.5^3+10.25=\frac{5^4}{4}-125=\frac{125}{4}\end{aligned}$ $\begin{aligned} & \text { Range } \Rightarrow\left[\frac{29}{4}, 32\right] \Rightarrow 4(\alpha+\beta)=128+29 \\ & =157\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>a</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>a</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>.</mo><msub><mi>a</mi><mi>n</mi></msub></math> be an A.P. If the sum of its first four terms is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn></math> and the sum of its last four terms is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>170</mn></math> , then the product of its middle two terms is _____ .
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>a</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><msub><mi>a</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>,</mo><mo>…</mo><mo>.</mo><msub><mi>a</mi><mi>n</mi></msub></math> be an A.P. If the sum of its first four terms is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn></math> and the sum of its last four terms is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>170</mn></math> , then the product of its middle two terms is _____ .
solution:
Let the common difference of an A.P. be d, then

a1+a2+a3+a4=50

⇒4a+6d=50

⇒32+6d=50

⇒d=3

Also,

an-3+an-2+an-1+an=170

⇒4a+4n-10·d=170

⇒32+4n-10·3=170

⇒n=14

So, middle terms are 

a7=a+6d=26, a8=a+7d=29

⇒a7·a8=754

subject: Mathematics
q: $z$ and $w$ are two non zero complex no.s such that $|z|=|w|$ and $\operatorname{Arg} z+\operatorname{Arg} w=\pi$ then $z$ equals
question: $z$ and $w$ are two non zero complex no.s such that $|z|=|w|$ and $\operatorname{Arg} z+\operatorname{Arg} w=\pi$ then $z$ equals
solution: Let $|\mathrm{z}|=|\omega|=\mathrm{r} \quad \therefore \mathrm{z}=\mathrm{re}^{\mathrm{i} \theta}, \omega=\mathrm{re}^{\mathrm{i} \phi}$ where $\theta+\phi=\pi \quad \therefore \bar{\omega}=\mathrm{re}^{-\mathrm{i \phi}}$ $\therefore z=\mathrm{re}^{i(\pi-\phi)}=\mathrm{re}^{\mathrm{i} \pi} \cdot \mathrm{e}^{-i \phi}=-\mathrm{re}^{-i \phi}=-\bar{\omega}$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let the equation of plane passing through the line of intersection of the planes x+2y+az=2 and x−y+z=3 be 5x−11y+bz=6a−1. For c∈ℤ, if the distance of this plane from the point a,−c,c is 2a, then a+bc is equal to
question: Let the equation of plane passing through the line of intersection of the planes x+2y+az=2 and x−y+z=3 be 5x−11y+bz=6a−1. For c∈ℤ, if the distance of this plane from the point a,−c,c is 2a, then a+bc is equal to
solution:
Let the equation of plane passing through the intersection of two given planes:

x+2y+az-2+λ(x-y+z-3)=0

⇒x(λ+1)+y(2-λ)+z(a+λ)-2-3λ=0

This is same as 5x-11y+bz=6a-1

λ+15=2-λ-11=a+λb=2+3λ6a-1

⇒-11λ-11=10-5λ

⇒6λ=-21⇒λ=-72

2-λ-11=2+3λ6a-1⇒2+72-11=2-2126a-1⇒-12=-172(6a-1)⇒6a-1=17⇒a=3

2-λ-11=a+λb⇒-12=3-72b

⇒-b2=-12⇒b=1

∴ point (a,-c,c)≡(3,-c,c)

Distance=2a=23

Plane:5 x-11 y+z=17

The distance of the plane from (3,-c,c) is

15+11c+c-17147=23

⇒|12c-2|=14

⇒c=-1,43

c=-1       c∈ℤ

∴a+bc=3+1-1=-4

Hence this is the required option.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let $f_{k}(x)=\frac{1}{k}\left(\sin ^{k} x+\cos ^{k} x\right)$ for $\mathrm{k}=1,2,3, \ldots$ Then for all $\mathrm{x} \in \mathrm{R},$ the value of $f_{4}(x)-f_{6}(x)$ is equal to :
question: Let $f_{k}(x)=\frac{1}{k}\left(\sin ^{k} x+\cos ^{k} x\right)$ for $\mathrm{k}=1,2,3, \ldots$ Then for all $\mathrm{x} \in \mathrm{R},$ the value of $f_{4}(x)-f_{6}(x)$ is equal to :
solution:
$f_{k}(x)=\frac{1}{k}\left(\sin ^{k} x+\cos ^{k} x\right)$

$f_{4}(x)=\frac{1}{4}\left[\sin ^{4} x+\cos ^{4} x\right]$

$=\frac{1}{4}\left[\left(\sin ^{2} x+\cos ^{2} x\right)^{2}-\frac{(\sin 2 x)^{2}}{2}\right]$

$=\frac{1}{4}\left[1-\frac{(\sin 2 x)^{2}}{2}\right]$

$f_{6}(x)=\frac{1}{6}\left[\sin ^{6} x+\cos ^{6} x\right]$

$=\frac{1}{6}\left[\left(\sin ^{2} x+\left(\cos ^{2} x\right)-\frac{3}{4}\left(\sin ^{2} x\right)^{2}\right]\right.$

$=\frac{1}{6}\left[1-\frac{3}{4}(\sin 2 x)^{2}\right]$

Now $f_{4}(x)-f_{(6)}(x)=\frac{1}{4}-\frac{1}{6}-\frac{(\sin 2 x)^{2}}{8}+\frac{1}{8}(\sin 2 x)^{2}$

$=\frac{1}{12}$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced></math> be a cubic polynomial with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mn>1</mn></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>10</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>,</mo></math> and has a local minima at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>f</mi><mo>'</mo></msup><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced></math> has a local minima at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo></math> Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mn>3</mn></mfenced></math> is equal to <u>              </u> .
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced></math> be a cubic polynomial with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mn>1</mn></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>10</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>,</mo></math> and has a local minima at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>f</mi><mo>'</mo></msup><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced></math> has a local minima at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo></math> Then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mn>3</mn></mfenced></math> is equal to <u>              </u> .
solution:
Let, fx=ax3+bx2+cx+d

Diff w. r. t.  'x'

f'x=3ax2+2bx+c

Again diff. w. r. t.  'x'

f"x=6ax+2b

f"-1=0

-6a+2b=0

b=3a

f'1=0

3a+6a+c=0

c=-9a

f1=-10 

-5a+d=-10      …i

f(-1)=6 

11a+d=6       …ii

From equations i-ii

We get a=1, d=-5, b=3, c=-9

Then, fx=x3+3x2-9x-5

Hence,  f3=27+27-27-5

f3=22

subject: Mathematics
q:
Let $(\alpha, \beta, \gamma)$ be the co-ordinates of the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the point $(5,4,2)$ on the line $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=(-\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-\hat{k})$.
Then the length of the projection of the vector $\alpha \hat{i}+\beta \hat{j}+\gamma \hat{k}$ on the vector $6 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$ is :
question:
Let $(\alpha, \beta, \gamma)$ be the co-ordinates of the foot of the perpendicular drawn from the point $(5,4,2)$ on the line $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}=(-\hat{i}+3 \hat{j}+\hat{k})+\lambda(2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-\hat{k})$.
Then the length of the projection of the vector $\alpha \hat{i}+\beta \hat{j}+\gamma \hat{k}$ on the vector $6 \hat{i}+2 \hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$ is :
solution:
Line: $\vec{r} = (-1, 3, 1) + \lambda(2, 3, -1)$, Point P = (5, 4, 2).
Any point on line: $Q = (-1 + 2\lambda, 3 + 3\lambda, 1 - \lambda)$.
$\vec{PQ} = (-6 + 2\lambda, -1 + 3\lambda, -1 - \lambda)$.
For perpendicular: $\vec{PQ} \cdot (2, 3, -1) = 0$ gives $14\lambda - 14 = 0$, so $\lambda = 1$.
Foot $(\alpha, \beta, \gamma) = (1, 6, 0)$.
Projection of $(1, 6, 0)$ on $(6, 2, 3)$: $\frac{|6 + 12 + 0|}{\sqrt{49}} = \frac{18}{7}$.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math><mi>α</mi><mtext> </mtext><mo>∈</mo><mi>R</mi></math> and the three vectors <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>a</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mi>α</mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo><mtext>  </mtext><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>b</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mi>α</mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>c</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mi>α</mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>.</mo></math> Then the set S = {<math><mi>α</mi><mo>:</mo><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>a</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>,</mo><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>b</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> and <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>c</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> are coplanar}
question: Let <math><mi>α</mi><mtext> </mtext><mo>∈</mo><mi>R</mi></math> and the three vectors <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>a</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mi>α</mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>,</mo><mtext>  </mtext><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>b</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mi>α</mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> and <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>c</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mi>α</mi><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>.</mo></math> Then the set S = {<math><mi>α</mi><mo>:</mo><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>a</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>,</mo><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>b</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> and <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>c</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover></math> are coplanar}
options: contains exactly two numbers only one of which is positive
solution:
If a→, b→ and c→ are coplanar then, 
α1321-αα-23=0
⇒3α2+18=0
⇒α∈ϕ

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of ways, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn></math> identical cubes, of which <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>11</mn></math> are blue and rest are red, can be placed in a row so that between any two red cubes there should be at least <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> blue cubes, is ______.
question: The number of ways, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn></math> identical cubes, of which <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>11</mn></math> are blue and rest are red, can be placed in a row so that between any two red cubes there should be at least <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn></math> blue cubes, is ______.
solution:
Given, 4 blue & 5 red spherical ball

Now according to question there should be minimum 2 blue ball in between any two red ball.

So assume a,b,c,d,e,f are number of blue balls,

So, a+b+c+d+e+f=11  ......(i)

Where b,c,d,e⩾2

Now let b=b'+2

c=c'+2

d=d'+2

e=e'+2

So equation (i) becomes

a+b'+2+c'+2+d'+2+e'+2+f=11

⇒ a+b'+c'+d'+e'+f=3

Now total ways will be

= 3+6-1C6-1=8C5=8C3=56

subject: Mathematics
q: The number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math>-digit natural numbers, such that the product of their digits is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>36</mn></math>, is
question: The number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math>-digit natural numbers, such that the product of their digits is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>36</mn></math>, is
solution:
Let the five digit number be abcde

∵   36=22×32

Case I: When exactly one digit is 1

So, number of permutations of 2,2,3,3,1 will be

 =5!2!2!=30

Case II: When exactly two digits are 1

So, number of permutations of 4,3,3,1,1,6,2,3,1,1 or 9,2,2,1,1 will be

=5!2!2!+5!2!+5!2!2!=120

Case III: When exactly three digits are 1

So, number of permutations of 4,9,1,1,1 or 6,6,1,1,1 will be

=5!3!+5!3!2!=30

Hence, the required numbers=30+120+30=180

subject: Mathematics
q: If the shortest distance between the line joining the points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>, and the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>z</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow><mn>0</mn></mfrac></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>28</mn><msup><mi>α</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is equal to _____ .
question: If the shortest distance between the line joining the points <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>, and the line <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>y</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>z</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow><mn>0</mn></mfrac></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>28</mn><msup><mi>α</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> is equal to _____ .
solution:
Equation of line joining 1,2,3 and 2,3,4 is

r→=i^+2j^+3k^+λi^+j^+k^

Also, vector equation is

r→=i^-j^+2k^+μ2i^-j^

So,

a→1=i^+2j^+3k^

a→2=i^-j^+2k^

Hence,

a→2-a→1=0i^-3j^-k^

And,

b→1=i^+j^+k^

b→2=2i^-j^+0k^

And, 

b→1×b→2=i^j^k^1112-10=i^+2j^-3k^

⇒b→1×b→2=14

Required shortest distance is

α=a→2-a→1·b→1×b→2b→1×b→2

⇒α=-3j^-k^·i^+2j^-3k^14

⇒α=-6+314=314

Now, 28α2=228×914=18.

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mi>Q</mi></math> be two vertical poles, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>160</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> apart from each other. Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> be the middle point of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math>, which are feet of these two poles. Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>8</mn></mfrac></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> be the angles of elevation from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>, respectively. If the height of pole <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mi>Q</mi></math> is twice the height of pole <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>tan</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>θ</mi></math> is equal to
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mi>Q</mi></math> be two vertical poles, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>160</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> apart from each other. Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> be the middle point of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Q</mi></math>, which are feet of these two poles. Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>8</mn></mfrac></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> be the angles of elevation from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math>, respectively. If the height of pole <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mi>Q</mi></math> is twice the height of pole <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mi>B</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>tan</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>θ</mi></math> is equal to
solution:
From the given information in the question we can draw the following diagram

Let BC=CQ=x & AB=h and PQ=2h

So tanθ=hx and tanπ8=2 hx

⇒tanθtanπ8=12

⇒tanθ=122-1

⇒tan2θ=143-22

q: Let the system of equations x+2y+3z=5,2x+3y+z=9,4x+3y+λz=μ have infinite number of solutions. Then λ+2μ is equal to:
question: Let the system of equations x+2y+3z=5,2x+3y+z=9,4x+3y+λz=μ have infinite number of solutions. Then λ+2μ is equal to:
solution:
Given: x+2y+3z=5, 2x+3y+z=9 and 4x+3y+λz=μ.

For infinite solutions, Δ=Δ1=Δ2=Δ3=0.

⇒Δ=12323143λ=0

⇒3λ-3-4λ+8+18-36=0

⇒λ=−13

Now, finding 

⇒Δ1=523931μ3−13=0

⇒μ=15

Now, finding

⇒Δ2=1532914μλ

⇒Δ2=153291415−13

⇒Δ2=0

And 

⇒Δ3=12523943μ

⇒Δ3=1252394315

⇒Δ3=0

For λ=−13, μ=15 system of equation has infinite solutions.

⇒λ+2μ=17.

q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></math> be real constants such that the function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi></math> defined by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="" open="{"><mtable><mtr><mtd><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo></mtd><mtd><mi>x</mi><mo>≤</mo><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mi>b</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo></mtd><mtd><mi>x</mi><mo>></mo><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced></math> be differentiable on <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>. question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">b</mi></math> be real constants such that the function <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi></math> defined by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfenced close="" open="{"><mtable><mtr><mtd><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo></mtd><mtd><mi>x</mi><mo>≤</mo><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr><mtr><mtd><mi>b</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>,</mo></mtd><mtd><mi>x</mi><mo>></mo><mn>1</mn></mtd></mtr></mtable></mfenced></math> be differentiable on <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>. Then, the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msubsup><mo>∫</mo><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow><mn>2</mn></msubsup><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mi>d</mi><mi>x</mi></math> equals
solution:
Given: fx=x2+3x+a,x≤1bx+2,x>1

Now, for function to be continuous,

⇒12+3×1+a=b×1+2

⇒4+a=b+2

⇒a-b=-2   ...i

Now, differentiating and putting the value of x,

 ⇒2x+3=b

⇒21+3=b

⇒b=5

⇒a=3

⇒∫-22fxdx=∫-21x2+3x+adx+∫12bx+2dx

⇒∫-22fxdx=∫-21x2+3x+3dx+∫125x+2dx

⇒∫-22fxdx=x33+3x22+3x-21+5x22+2x12

⇒∫-22fxdx=13+32+3--83+6-6+5×42+2×2-52+2

⇒∫-22fxdx=13+32+3+83+14-92

⇒∫-22fxdx=152+14-92

⇒∫-22fxdx=17

q: The plane containing the line $\frac{x-3}{2}=\frac{y+2}{-1}=\frac{z-1}{3}$ and also containing its projection on the plane $2 \mathrm{x}+3 \mathrm{y}-\mathrm{z}=5$, contains which one of the following points?
question: The plane containing the line $\frac{x-3}{2}=\frac{y+2}{-1}=\frac{z-1}{3}$ and also containing its projection on the plane $2 \mathrm{x}+3 \mathrm{y}-\mathrm{z}=5$, contains which one of the following points?
solution:
Let normal to the required plane is $\vec{n}$

$\Rightarrow \vec{n}$ is perpendicular to both vector $2 \hat{i}-\hat{j}+3 \hat{k}$ and

$2 \hat{i}+3 \hat{j}-3 \hat{k}$

$\Rightarrow \quad \vec{n}=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}\hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 2 & -1 & 3 \\ 2 & 3 & -1\end{array}\right|=-8 \hat{i}+8 \hat{j}+8 \hat{k}$

$\Rightarrow$ Equation of the required plane is $\Rightarrow \quad(x-3)(-8)+(y+2) \times 8+(z-1) \times 8=0$

$\Rightarrow \quad(x-3)(-1)+(y+2) \times 1+(z-1) \times 1=0$

$\Rightarrow x-3-y-2-z+1=0$

$\because \quad x-y-z=4$ passes through (2,0,-2)

$\therefore \quad$ plane contains (2,0,-2)

question: If the area (in sq. units) of the region <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="{" close="}" separators="|"><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>y</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>≤</mo><mn>4</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>y</mi><mo>≤</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>≥</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>y</mi><mo>≥</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> is <math><mi>a</mi><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt><mo>+</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>,</mo></math> then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>b</mi></math> is equal to
solution:
The given equations are,

y2=4x & x+y=1

⇒y2=41-y

⇒y2+4y-4=0 ⇒y=22-2

∴ Required Area =∫022-21-y-y24dx=y-y22-y312022-2

=22-2-22-222-22-2312-0

=22-21-2-1-2-123=-103+823

⇒-103+823=a2+b ⇒a=83, b=-103
∴ a-b=83+103=6

If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mn>8</mn></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>7</mn></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mn>14</mn></mfenced></math> is equal to:
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mo>:</mo><mi>R</mi><mo>→</mo><mi>R</mi></math> be a continuous function such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>-</mo><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>x</mi></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mn>8</mn></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>7</mn></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>f</mi><mfenced><mn>14</mn></mfenced></math> is equal to:
solution:
Given f3x-fx=x

Now replance x by x3  infinite times,

fx-fx3=x3

fx3-fx32=x32..

On adding, we get

fx-limn→∞fx3n=x13+132+⋯+∞

fx-limn→∞fx3n=x131-13=x2

⇒  fx-f0=x2

Given f8=7

so f8-f0=4

i.e. f0=3

∴  fx=x2+3

Hence f14=10

question: The number of ways five alphabets can be chosen from the alphabets of the word MATHEMATICS, where the chosen alphabets are not necessarily distinct, is equal to :
solution:
AA, MM, TT, H, I, C, S, E
(1) All distinct
${ }^8 \mathrm{C}_5 \rightarrow 56$
(2) 2 same, 3 different
${ }^3 \mathrm{C}_1 \times{ }^7 \mathrm{C}_3 \rightarrow 105$
(3) 2 same $I^{\text {st }}$ kind, 2 same $2^{\text {nd }}$ kind, 1 different
${ }^3 \mathrm{C}_2 \times{ }^6 \mathrm{C}_1 \rightarrow 18$
Total $\rightarrow 179$

question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><munder><mi>lim</mi><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>→</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></munder><mfrac><mrow><mi>a</mi><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>e</mi><mi>x</mi></msup><mo>−</mo><mi>b</mi><msub><mi>log</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><mi>c</mi><mi>x</mi><msup><mi>e</mi><mrow><mo>−</mo><mi>x</mi></mrow></msup></mrow><mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mi>sin</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>a</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>b</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>c</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to ______.
solution:
Given: 

limx→0ax2ex-bloge1+x+cxe-xx2sinx=1

⇒limx→0ax2ex-bloge1+x+cxe-xx2·x·sinxx=1

⇒limx→0ax21+x1!+x22!+...-bx-x22+x33+...+cx1-x1!+x22!+...x2×x=1

Coefficient of x=0

⇒-b+c=0   ...iii

Coefficient of x2=0

⇒a+b2-c=0   ...ii

⇒a-c2=0

⇒a=c2

Coefficient of x3=1

⇒a-b3+c2=1   ...iii

⇒c2-c3+c2=1

⇒2c3=1

⇒c=32=b

⇒a=34

⇒16a2+b2+c2=16916+94×2

⇒16a2+b2+c2=81

q:
Consider the following two statements:

P : If 7 is an odd number, then 7 is divisible by 2.

Q : If 7 is a prime number, then 7 is an odd number.

If V1 is the truth value of the contrapositive of P and V2 is the truth value of contrapositive of Q, then the ordered pair V1,V2 equals
question:
Consider the following two statements:

P : If 7 is an odd number, then 7 is divisible by 2.

Q : If 7 is a prime number, then 7 is an odd number.

If V1 is the truth value of the contrapositive of P and V2 is the truth value of contrapositive of Q, then the ordered pair V1,V2 equals
solution:
Contrapositive of P:

7 is not divisible by 2⇒7 is not odd number

T ⇒F:FV1

Contrapositive of Q:

7 is not odd number ⇒7 is not a prime number  

F⇒ F:T V2

Thus, V1,V2≡F,T

q: The sum to 20 terms of the series 2·22-32+2·42-52+2·62-............ is equal to__________.
question: The sum to 20 terms of the series 2·22-32+2·42-52+2·62-............ is equal to__________.
solution:
Given,

2·22-32+2·42-52+2·62-............20 terms,

=∑r=1102·(2r)2-(2r+1)2

=∑r=1108r2-4r2-4r-1

=∑r=1104r2-4r-1

=4∑r=110r2-4∑r=110r-∑r=1101

=4·10·11·216-410·112-10

=44·35-220-10=1540-230=1310

q: Let <math><mi>f</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">sin</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mrow><mo>+</mo><mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">cos</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mrow><mo>.</mo></math> Then, <math><mi>f</mi></math> is an increasing function in the interval:
question: Let <math><mi>f</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">sin</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mrow><mo>+</mo><mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">cos</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mi>x</mi></mrow></mrow><mo>.</mo></math> Then, <math><mi>f</mi></math> is an increasing function in the interval:
solution:
fx=sin4⁡x+cos4⁡x

f′x=4sin3⁡xcos⁡x+4cos3⁡x-sin⁡x

=4sin⁡xcos⁡xsin2⁡x-cos2⁡x

= -2sin⁡2xcos⁡2x

= -sin⁡4x

fx, is increasing when f′x>0

⇒ -sin⁡4x>0

⇒ sin⁡4x<0

⇒π<4x<2π

⇒π4<x<π2
⇒ x ∈π4,π2

subject: Mathematics
q: The locus of mid-points of the line segments joining <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and the points on the ellipse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mn>9</mn></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> is :
question: The locus of mid-points of the line segments joining <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mo>,</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> and the points on the ellipse <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mn>4</mn></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><msup><mi>y</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mn>9</mn></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> is :
solution:
Parametric point on the given ellipse is 2sinθ, 3cosθ

Let, the mid-point of line segments joining -3,-5 and 2sinθ,3cosθ is h, k

Then, by using mid-point formula, we get

2sinθ-32=h, 3cosθ-52=k

⇒2sinθ=2h+3, 3cosθ=2k+5

⇒sinθ=2h+32, cosθ=2k+53

We know, sin2θ+cos2θ=1

⇒2h+322+2k+532=1

⇒144h2+9+12h+194k2+25+20k=1

⇒36h2+16k2+108h+80k+145=0

Hence, the locus of x,y is 36x2+16y2+108x+80y+145=0

q: If <math><mi>λ</mi></math> be the ratio of the roots of the quadratic equation in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>m</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>m</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, then the least value of <math><mi>m</mi></math> for which <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>λ</mi></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>, is :
question: If <math><mi>λ</mi></math> be the ratio of the roots of the quadratic equation in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>m</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>m</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfenced><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, then the least value of <math><mi>m</mi></math> for which <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>λ</mi><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mi>λ</mi></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math>, is :
solution:
Let α and β are the roots of given equation 3m2x2+mm-4x+2=0.

∴α+β=-mm-43m2 &  αβ=23m2         .........i

Given αβ=λ    &   λ+1λ=1

⇒αβ+βα=1

⇒α+β2=3αβ

Using equation i, we get

-mm-43m22=3×23m2

⇒m=4±32

Hence, the least value of m is 4-32.

q: A man is walking towards a vertical pillar in a straight path, at a uniform speed. At a certain point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> on the path, he observes that the angle of elevation of the top of the pillar is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></msup></math>.  After walking for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> minutes from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> in the same direction, at a point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>, he observes that the angle of elevation of the top of the pillar is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></msup></math> . Then the time taken (in minutes) by him, from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> to reach the pillar, is
question: A man is walking towards a vertical pillar in a straight path, at a uniform speed. At a certain point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> on the path, he observes that the angle of elevation of the top of the pillar is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>30</mn><mo>°</mo></msup></math>.  After walking for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn></math> minutes from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> in the same direction, at a point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math>, he observes that the angle of elevation of the top of the pillar is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo></msup></math> . Then the time taken (in minutes) by him, from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> to reach the pillar, is
solution:
ΔQPA :hx+y=tan30°

⇒3h=x+y ......i

ΔQPB :hy=tan60°

⇒h= 3y   ......ii

From equation i & ii, we get

3y=x+y 

⇒y=x2

∵ Speed is uniform

Therefore, distance x walked by him in 10 min.

and distance x2 walked by him in 5 min.

solution:
∫x5fx3dx =∫x3.x2fx3dx  

Applying integration by parts, we get

=x3∫x2fx3dx-∫ddxx3∫x2fx3dx

=13x3ψx3-∫x2ψx3dx+c, where c is the constant of integration.

q: Let xi1≤i≤10 be ten observation of a random variable X. If ∑i=110xi-p=3 and ∑i=110xi-p2=9 where 0≠p∈R, then the standard deviation of these observations is:
question: Let xi1≤i≤10 be ten observation of a random variable X. If ∑i=110xi-p=3 and ∑i=110xi-p2=9 where 0≠p∈R, then the standard deviation of these observations is:
solution:
Standard Deviation S.D.=∑i=110xi-p210-∑i=110xi-p102

=910-3102=910

subject: Mathematics
q:
If $z^2+z+1=0$, where $z$ is a complex number, then the value of
$$
\left(z+\frac{1}{z}\right)^2+\left(z^2+\frac{1}{z^2}\right)^2+\left(z^3+\frac{1}{z^3}\right)^2+\cdots+\left(z^6+\frac{1}{z^6}\right)^2
$$
question:
If $z^2+z+1=0$, where $z$ is a complex number, then the value of
$$
\left(z+\frac{1}{z}\right)^2+\left(z^2+\frac{1}{z^2}\right)^2+\left(z^3+\frac{1}{z^3}\right)^2+\cdots+\left(z^6+\frac{1}{z^6}\right)^2
$$
solution: $z^2+z+1=0 \quad \Rightarrow z=\omega$ or $\omega^2$ so, $z+\frac{1}{z}=\omega+\omega^2=-1, z^2+\frac{1}{z^2}=\omega^2+\omega=-1, z^3+\frac{1}{z^3}=\omega^3+\omega^3=2$ $z^4+\frac{1}{z^4}=-1, z^5+\frac{1}{z^5}=-1$ and $z^6+\frac{1}{z^6}=2$ $\therefore$ The given sum $=1+1+4+1+1+4=12$
